meta.title: W220 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W220 engine — M112, M113, M137, M275, OM613, OM648, OM628. Real UK costs, reliability data, and the OM648 diesel that sets the standard. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes W220 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W220 engine typically costs £1,800–£12,000 supply only, depending on the engine — the OM648 diesel sits at £2,200–£4,500, while the M113 V8 is £2,500–£5,500 and the V12 M137/M275 is £5,000–£12,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which W220 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM648 3.2-litre straight-six diesel (facelift S 320 CDI) is the most reliable choice — it's Mercedes-developed, refined, and has a strong track record. The M113 5.0-litre V8 (S 500) is also very dependable. Avoid the M137 V12 (S 600/S 63 AMG) — it's one of Mercedes' least reliable engines.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the W220?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M137 V12 can suffer from cylinder wall deformation, leading to excessive oil consumption and eventual engine failure; the OM613 diesel (pre-facelift) can experience injector and EGR issues; the M113 V8 can develop oil leaks from valve cover and oil pan gaskets; and the M275 biturbo V12 can suffer from turbocharger failure. Regular servicing is key.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes W220 reliable overall?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on the engine. The M113 V8 and OM648 diesel are generally reliable and well-regarded. The M112 V6 is also solid. The M137 V12 and early OM613 diesel are less reliable and should be approached with caution. The M275 biturbo V12 is complex and costly to maintain.

mainEntity.name: Which W220 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M137 V12 (S 600, 2000–2002; S 63 AMG, 2001–2005) — it's one of Mercedes-Benz's least reliable engines ever made, with cylinder wall deformation being a common, catastrophic failure. The M275 biturbo V12 (facelift S 600) is also complex and costly, though more reliable than the M137. Any W220 without documented service history should be approached with caution.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W220 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Warning signs include excessive oil consumption and blue smoke (M137 V12), rough running or starting issues (OM613 injectors), visible oil leaks (M113 V8), or turbocharger whine/whistle (M275 biturbo). If you notice any of these, have the car inspected immediately — ignoring them can lead to a full engine failure.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes W220 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value and which engine. A well-kept W220 is worth £3,000–£10,000, so a £2,200–£4,500 OM648 recon is 22–150% of its value. On a sub-£4,000 W220, the recon may approach or exceed our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'd advise walking away. Against a well-kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to 28–56% — a repair decision, not a financial one. For V12 cars, replacement costs often exceed the vehicle's value entirely.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W220 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM648 diesel can easily exceed 200,000 miles with regular servicing. The M113 V8 is similarly durable. The M112 V6 can also reach high mileages. The M137 V12 is significantly less durable, with many failing before 100,000 miles. The M275 biturbo V12 can reach 100,000+ miles with meticulous maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W220 and the previous W140?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W220 is the fourth-generation S-Class, built on a lighter platform than the W140. It introduced COMAND, Keyless-Go, and a range of new engines including the M137 V12 and OM648 diesel. The W140 (1991–1998) used older M104, M120, and OM603 engines and is covered on its own generation page.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W220 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen), on the vehicle documentation, and stamped on the engine block. For the W220, common codes are M112 (V6 petrol), M113 (V8 petrol), M137 (V12 petrol, pre-facelift), M275 (V12 biturbo, facelift), OM613 (diesel straight-6, pre-facelift), OM648 (diesel straight-6, facelift), and OM628 (diesel V8). Tell us your variant and year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the fourth-generation S-Class flagship — from the V6 M112 and V8 M113 to the V12 M137 and the OM648 straight-six diesel. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the engine range that defined Mercedes' luxury saloon at the turn of the millennium. Backed by 880+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: * M112/M113 families: 85% confidence — well‑documented Mercedes V6/V8 engines; pattern data. * M137 family: 70% confidence — short production run; known reliability issues; pattern data. * M275: 75% confidence — complex twin-turbo V12; pattern data. * OM613/OM648: 80% confidence — well‑documented CDI engines; pattern data. * OM628: 70% confidence — rare V8 diesel; limited data.

overview.intro: The W220 is the fourth generation of the Mercedes‑Benz S‑Class, launched in 1998 as the successor to the W140. Built on a new platform that was slightly smaller externally than its predecessor but offered greater interior volume, it introduced a range of advanced technologies including the COMAND infotainment system and Keyless-Go. The engine range spanned from the 2.8‑litre M112 V6 to the 6.3‑litre M137 V12, with AMG variants delivering up to 444 bhp. A significant facelift arrived in 2002, bringing new engines including the M275 biturbo V12 and the improved OM648 straight-six diesel. Production ended in July 2005, succeeded by the W221.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W220 (shared with C215 CL-Class) • Predecessor: W140 (1991–1998) • Successor: W221 (2005–2013) • Sibling body styles in this generation: C215 (CL-Class coupé)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W220 S‑Class generated 880+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM648 diesel and M113 V8 as the most‑requested powertrains.

items.quote: Mercedes' improved 3.2‑litre straight‑six diesel, introduced with the 2002 facelift, replaced the problematic OM613 with a more refined and reliable unit. It delivers 204 bhp with strong low‑end torque and better fuel economy than its predecessor.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern, efficient diesel for high‑mileage luxury motoring.

items.quote: Mercedes' 5.0‑litre V8, mated to the 4MATIC all‑wheel drive system, delivers 306 bhp with a silky, effortless character. The M113 is one of Mercedes' most robust V8 engines, with fewer of the issues that plague the M137 V12.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 power and all‑weather capability.

items.quote: The M137 V12 has gone on to be regarded as one of Mercedes‑Benz's least reliable engines ever made. Along with a host of other issues, one of the main concerns is cylinder wall deformation, allowing oil to pass into the combustion chamber. The 6.3‑litre S 63 AMG variant is even more problematic.
items.whoItsFor: collectors with deep pockets and a specialist mechanic on speed dial.

items.engine: M275.950 (S 600, facelift) — twin‑turbo and cooling system
items.quote: The biturbo V12 is complex and costly to maintain. Turbocharger failure, cooling system leaks, and ignition coil issues are all common — and all expensive to repair.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V12 flagship status and can afford the premium running costs.

items.quote: The facelift diesel offers the best balance of performance, economy (up to 34 mpg), and reliability. It's the sweet spot in the W220 range.
items.whoItsFor: high‑mileage drivers and those who want S‑Class luxury without V12 running costs.

items.quote: The supercharged 5.4‑litre V8 delivers 360 bhp with genuine AMG performance. It's often overlooked in favour of the V12 models but offers a more reliable and engaging driving experience.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want AMG performance without the V12's reliability headaches.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Oil leaks, timing chain wear, MAF sensor failure
Valve cover gasket leaks, oil pan leaks

rows.ownershipVerdict: AMG Sweet Spot — powerful and reliable.
Avoid — one of Mercedes' least reliable engines.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Injector failure, EGR clogging, turbo issues

rows.ownershipVerdict: Rare Choice — hard to source parts.

economicsRule.title: The W220 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,200–£4,500 OM648 recon against a £3,000–£10,000 W220 sits at 22–150% of the car's value. On a sub‑£4,000 W220, that approaches or exceeds our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £8,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 28–56% — a repair decision, not a financial one. The V12 cars are a different proposition entirely — their replacement costs often exceed the vehicle's value.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W220 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S 320 CDI (OM648) — best balance of economy and reliability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W220 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S 600 (M137/M275) — V12 running costs are astronomical.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W220 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: S 320 CDI (OM648) — the sweet spot in the range.

problems.title: M137 V12 Cylinder Wall Deformation (S 600 / S 63 AMG)
problems.rootCause: The M137 V12 suffers from cylinder wall deformation, allowing oil to pass into the combustion chamber. This leads to excessive oil consumption, blue smoke, and eventually catastrophic engine failure. Along with a host of other issues, it has gone on to be regarded as one of Mercedes‑Benz's least reliable engines ever made.
problems.recommendation: On any M137‑powered W220, have a compression test and oil consumption check performed at every service. If oil consumption exceeds 1 litre per 1,000 miles, consider the car uneconomical to repair.

cta.label: Compare M137 engine prices for your W220 V12 →

problems.title: OM613 Injector & EGR Issues (pre‑facelift S 320 CDI)
problems.rootCause: The early OM613 CDI engine suffers from injector failure and EGR valve clogging, leading to rough running, poor economy, and potential engine damage.
problems.recommendation: On pre‑facelift S 320 CDI models, have the injectors tested at 80,000 miles — a £500–£1,200 repair is far cheaper than the £2,000–£4,000 replacement that follows if an injector fails catastrophically.

cta.label: Compare OM613 engine prices for your W220 diesel →

problems.title: M113 V8 Oil Leaks (S 430 / S 500)
problems.rootCause: The M113 V8 is generally robust, but valve cover gaskets and oil pan gaskets can degrade over time, leading to oil leaks.
problems.recommendation: If you notice oil spots on your driveway or a burning oil smell, have the gaskets inspected immediately — a £400–£800 repair is cheap insurance against a £2,500–£5,500 engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare M113 engine prices for your W220 V8 →

problems.title: M275 Biturbo V12 Turbocharger Failure (facelift S 600)
problems.rootCause: The twin‑turbo V12's turbochargers can suffer from oil feed line contamination and bearing wear, leading to premature failure. Cooling system leaks and ignition coil failures are also common.
problems.recommendation: On facelift S 600 models, stick to strict service intervals and use the correct high‑grade oil — a £1,500–£2,500 turbo rebuild is far cheaper than the £6,000–£12,000 replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

cta.label: Compare M275 engine prices for your W220 V12 →

replacementCosts.h2: W220 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — the W220 shares its platform with the C215 CL‑Class, so engine‑bay access and labour times are consistent across the range. Costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are W220 saloon only. The C215 CL‑Class shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.

mostRequestedEngines: 1. OM648 (3.2 straight‑six diesel) — most‑requested diesel in the W220 range
2. M113 (V8 petrol) — strong demand for the S 500
3. M275 (V12 biturbo) — niche enthusiast demand for the facelift S 600
mostCommonFailures: 1. M137 V12 cylinder wall deformation (S 600/S 63 AMG)
